name: video-use
readme_summary: video-use is an open-source Python tool that leverages AI coding agents to automate video editing tasks. It processes raw footage by cutting filler words, applying color grading, adding audio fades, burning in customizable subtitles, and generating animation overlays, all while self-evaluating the output.
key_features: Cuts filler words and dead space
primary_use_case: Automating video editing through natural language commands processed by AI coding agents.
cover_image_prompt: A skilled video editor, depicted as a conductor leading an orchestra, stands before a digital console. Instead of musical notes, streams of raw video footage flow into the console from one side. The conductor gestures, and the console transforms the footage into a polished final video, complete with dynamic text overlays and smooth transitions, which flows out the other side. Subtle visual cues like waveform patterns and code snippets are integrated into the console's design. The scene is set in a modern, minimalist studio with soft, focused lighting, rendered in a 3D isometric illustration style with vibrant, clean colors, emphasizing the seamless transformation of raw footage into a professional edit.

name: ratty
description: A GPU-rendered terminal emulator with inline 3D graphics 🐀🧀
readme_summary: Ratty is a GPU-accelerated terminal emulator built with Rust and Ratatui, offering traditional 2D text rendering alongside a novel 3D mode. It supports inline 3D objects and image rendering via the Kitty Graphics Protocol, providing a unique visual experience for terminal applications.
primary_use_case: To provide an enhanced and visually rich terminal experience by enabling 3D graphics and GPU-accelerated rendering within the terminal interface.
cover_image_prompt: A whimsical, stylized 3D isometric illustration of a classic computer terminal. Instead of a flat screen, the terminal's display is a dynamic, glowing portal showing a vibrant, spinning 3D model of a rat. Around the portal, abstract geometric shapes and data streams flow, representing the GPU-rendered 3D graphics. Subtle code snippets are etched into the terminal's casing, and the overall mood is futuristic yet playful, with a color palette of deep blues, purples, and glowing neon accents. The repository name 'Ratty' is subtly integrated into a futuristic UI element on the terminal.

name: lay-public
description: Double Shift layout rescue for Linux/GNOME Wayland
readme_summary: Lay is a Rust-based utility for Linux desktops, primarily GNOME Wayland, that corrects typing errors caused by incorrect keyboard layouts. It allows users to fix the last typed word by double-pressing the Shift key, seamlessly switching it to the intended layout without manual copy-pasting.
key_features: Double Shift to fix the last word typed in the wrong layout
Works directly in applications without clipboard use
Supports GNOME Wayland via a Shell extension
Experimental support for KDE/Plasma and X11 backends
Local processing, no cloud or network requests
primary_use_case: Correcting the last typed word to the correct keyboard layout (e.g., Russian/English) with a double Shift press on Linux desktops, especially GNOME Wayland.
cover_image_prompt: A whimsical scene depicting a writer struggling with a jumbled keyboard. One key is glowing red, representing a mistyped word. Suddenly, a friendly digital assistant, shaped like a stylized 'S' for Shift, appears and taps the keyboard twice. The mistyped word instantly untangles and reassembles itself correctly on the paper. Subtle digital particles flow from the 'S' assistant to the keyboard. The background shows a clean, modern desktop interface with a hint of the 'lay' logo. The style is a 3D isometric illustration with bright, friendly colors and clear, readable elements.

name: hackingtool
description: ALL IN ONE Hacking Tool For Hackers
readme_summary: HackingTool is a comprehensive, all-in-one Python-based toolkit designed for security researchers and penetration testers. It aggregates over 185 tools across 20 categories, offering functionalities for web attacks, password attacks, wireless attacks, and more, with features like smart search, tag filtering, and automated installation.
key_features: Supports web attacks, password attacks, wireless attacks, etc.
Search functionality by name, description, or keyword
Tag filtering (osint, web, c2, cloud, mobile)
Tool recommendation based on user intent
Install status indicator for each tool
primary_use_case: To provide a unified and easy-to-use interface for accessing and managing a wide array of hacking and penetration testing tools for security professionals.
cover_image_prompt: A skilled artisan in a bustling workshop, surrounded by a vast array of specialized tools neatly organized on shelves and workbenches. The artisan is holding a master key, which glows with digital energy, and is using it to unlock a complex, multi-layered digital lock. Input streams of data flow into the lock, and successful unlocking reveals a clear pathway through a stylized network. Subtle UI elements showing tool categories and search bars are integrated into the workshop's design. The scene is rendered in a vibrant, 3D isometric illustration style, emphasizing organization, comprehensiveness, and the power of having all tools in one place.

name: davinci-resolve-mcp
description: MCP server integration for DaVinci Resolve
readme_summary: This Python-based MCP server provides comprehensive integration with the DaVinci Resolve Scripting API, enabling AI assistants to control post-production workflows. It offers 30 context-efficient tools for tasks like timeline editing, media management, rendering, and color grading.
key_features: Complete coverage of DaVinci Resolve Scripting API
Integration with AI assistants (Claude, Cursor, Windsurf)
Guarded kernel workflows for safe operations (probing, reporting, dry-runs)
Support for timeline editing, media pool management, render/deliver, review annotations, color/grade, Fusion composition, conform/interchange, audio/Fairlight, and project lifecycle management.
primary_use_case: To enable AI assistants to control and automate post-production workflows within DaVinci Resolve through natural language commands.
cover_image_prompt: A skilled conductor standing at a podium, orchestrating a complex symphony of video editing elements. Instead of musical notes, glowing lines of code and data streams flow from the conductor's baton towards various DaVinci Resolve interface elements like a timeline, color wheels, and render queues, bringing them to life. The conductor's score is a tablet displaying natural language commands. Subtle digital particles and the repository name 'davinci-resolve-mcp' integrated into the podium. The scene is a modern, dimly lit studio with a focus on the conductor and the illuminated interface elements. 3D isometric illustration style with a vibrant, yet focused color palette.
